title: Offshore Energy Security Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Offshore Energy Security Act of 2025 requires the government to hold oil and gas lease sales in the Gulf of Mexico twice a year for ten years starting in March 2026. Each sale must offer at least 74 million acres of ocean area where companies can drill for oil and gas. The bill also bans oil and gas drilling in areas near Florida's coast and in the Atlantic Ocean until 2035 to protect those waters. The law includes rules to prevent court cases from stopping or delaying these lease sales.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Offshore Energy Security Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Offshore Oil and Gas Lease Sales
sections.text: This section explains what offshore lease sales are and sets rules for them. An offshore lease sale is when the Secretary of the Interior sells the right to drill for oil and gas in ocean areas controlled by the federal government. These sales must follow the same rules and terms as a specific sale that happened in November two thousand twenty three in the Gulf of Mexico called Sale two hundred sixty one. If companies make acceptable bids on any area offered in the sale, the government must give out the leases within ninety days to the highest bidders. The Secretary of the Interior is the person in charge of these sales. The Secretary can skip other rules that would slow down approving these lease sales. Even though there is already a plan for oil and gas leasing from two thousand twenty four to two thousand twenty nine, the Secretary must hold at least twenty offshore lease sales during the ten years after this law takes effect. The Secretary should try to follow a decision that was approved on January seventeenth two thousand seventeen when conducting these sales. The Secretary must hold lease sales by specific dates starting March thirty first two thousand twenty six and then every six months after that, with the last sale by August thirty first two thousand thirty five. Each sale must offer at least seventy four million acres for lease in the Gulf of Mexico area that was identified in a two thousand sixteen government document. If someone sues about the environmental review of a lease sale, the lawsuit cannot cancel leases that were already given out or delay permits to drill, except in certain cases. If a court finds problems with the environmental review, the court cannot throw out the lease sale but must send it back to the Secretary to fix the problems. The Secretary must keep processing drilling permit applications. The Secretary must tell lease holders about any lawsuits within sixty days, and lease holders can ask to pause their lease terms while the lawsuit is ongoing. This section also changes an existing law about the Eastern Gulf of Mexico. It extends a ban on oil and gas leasing in that area until December thirty first two thousand thirty five and adds the South Atlantic and Straits of Florida areas to the ban. However, the ban does not affect leases that already exist and the Secretary can still give out leases in these areas for environmental protection purposes like shore protection and habitat restoration.
